_comment: Distilled from the matching pair in PM JAY/Standard Treatment Guideline/master/<condition>/ : _Medco.pdf (clinical + mandatory docs) + _PPD.CPD.pdf (PPD/CPD processing checklists). Filename = procedure_code. This is what the Documentation Checker, Query Predictor, and Medical Justification agents read AFTER the picker has chosen the code.
clinical_indications: Tumors leading to CSF blockage of the lateral ventricles, third ventricle, and those in the posterior fossa blocking the cerebral aqueduct or fourth ventricle
Communicating hydrocephalus secondary to meningitis or subarachnoid hemorrhage
Myelomeningocele causing hydrocephalus due to hindbrain malformation
Dandy-Walker syndrome with cystic deformity of the fourth ventricle, hypoplasia of the cerebellar vermis, and an enlarged posterior fossa
Arachnoid cysts causing hydrocephalus due to trapped CSF collection
Idiopathic intracranial hypertension leading to rise in intracranial pressure and potential vision loss
Normal-pressure hydrocephalus causing memory problems/dementia, gait dysfunction, and urinary incontinence

preauth.label: Clinical Notes including evaluation findings and planned line of treatment

claim.label: Post Procedure clinical photograph/ Scar photograph (Optional)

ppd_preauth.q: Was CT/ MRI brain report of patient submitted?

common_queries: CT/MRI brain report not submitted at pre-authorization.
Clinical notes detailing history, signs, symptoms, indication, and planned treatment not submitted at pre-authorization.
Detailed Indoor Case Papers (ICPs) not submitted at claim submission.
Detailed Procedure/ Operation notes not submitted at claim submission.
Detailed Discharge Summary not submitted at claim submission.

clinical_key_pointers: Congenital hydrocephalus after aqueductal stenosis is a genetic disorder which can cause deformations of the nervous system and is associated with mental retardation, abducted thumbs, and spastic paraplegia
Tumors leading to CSF blockage of the lateral ventricles, third ventricle, and those in the posterior fossa blocking the cerebral aqueduct or fourth ventricle
Communicating hydrocephalus secondary to meningitis or subarachnoid hemorrhage
Myelomeningocele causes the development of hydrocephalus because the flow of CSF is altered due to hindbrain malformation
Craniosynostosis occurs when the sutures of the skull close too early with sutures fusing before the brain stops growing and in rare occasions can cause hydrocephalus
Dandy-Walker syndrome presents with a cystic deformity of the fourth ventricle, hypoplasia of the cerebellar vermis, and an enlarged posterior fossa
Arachnoid cysts are a defect caused when CSF forms a collection that is trapped in the arachnoid membranes resulting in a block of the normal flow of CSF resulting in hydrocephalus

Idiopathic intracranial hypertension is a rare neurological disorder affecting approximately 1 in 100,000 people, usually women of childbearing age, causing a rise in intracranial pressure which can result in permanent loss of vision
Normal-pressure hydrocephalus caused a classic triad of memory problems/dementia, gait dysfunction, and urinary incontinence.
